data.title: The Trade Desk: Potential Value Trap - Recovery Likely Lumpy And Prolonged (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
I previously covered The Trade Desk, Inc. (TTD) in August 2025, discussing how its independent DSP story had not played out as expected, with Kokai/OpenPath yet to be accretive to its FQ2'25 performance and FQ3'25 guidance.

Despite the ongoing underperformance against other walled gardens/DSP players that had been reporting high double-digit growths, I had believed that the ad tech company remained compelling then, thanks to the cheaper valuations, the lack of debt, and the promising early Kokai results in FQ2'25 - resulting in my reiterated Buy rating.

In this article, I shall discuss why I am admitting defeat and downgrading the TTD stock to a Hold here, as my prior Buy ratings fail to perform to expectations, attributed to the intensifying competition, the decelerating growth profile across FQ3'25 results/FQ4'25 guidance, and the potential value trap.

TTD Faces Intensifying Competition From AMZN

TTD 1Y Stock Price

Since my last Buy rating, the bears have proven my bullish thesis wrong indeed, with TTD breaching its 5Y established floor at the $41s ranges as discussed in my last article and the stock already hitting a new 52-week low of $38s by the time of writing.

This development may potentially be attributed to the market's mounting uncertainties surrounding its future ad-tech prospects, attributed to the commencement of Amazon DSP's (AMZN) partnership with its streaming partners, including:

  • The Walt Disney Company (DIS) from Q3'25 onwards,
  • exclusive partnership - Roku (ROKU) from Q4'25 onwards, and
  • Netflix (NFLX) from Q4'25 onwards.

At a time when AMZN already reports outsized FQ3'25 advertising growth by +23.5% YoY against the tougher comparison from a year ago at +18.8% YoY, it is unsurprising that TTD has further retraced post FQ3'25 earnings call, attributed to its notably decelerating growth profile at +17.6% YoY and +27.3% YoY, respectively.

This development is unsurprising indeed, since AMZN now boasts an expanded exposure to the CTV advertising end market across four of its partners (including Prime Video) at an estimated 26% of the total TV viewing share and 56.8% of the total streaming share as of October 2025.

I am of the opinion that AMZN's growing DSP partnerships are likely to trigger headwinds to TTD's future ad-tech/CTV monetization cadence, attributed to the potentially reduced ad inventory and the narrowed advertiser base, since AMZN and ROKU comprise "more than 80% of U.S. CTV households according to ComScore data."

This is on top of AMZN's extremely aggressive pricing approach, as they reportedly "charges 0% for programmatic guaranteed deals on their owned media and 1% on open-web buys, far below TTD’s estimated 20% take rate" worsened by AMZN's "deepening partnership with Omnicom following its US media account win. Amazon is positioned as the most credible challenger TTD has faced in years."

The pricing headwind builds upon TTD's system issues as advertisers reportedly highlighted that "Kokai is less stable and more complex than Solimar—problems magnified during a quarter (Q4'25 holiday season) where execution errors carry outsized cost," attributed to numerous bugs such as "failed launches and missing audiences to broken integrations."

Given the higher outlays and the operational issues for the Kokai transition, it is unsurprising that TTD is currently at a great disadvantage, as Alphabet (GOOG) also "matches Amazon’s close-to-zero rates" while seemingly triggering the ongoing "race to the bottom," attributed to the former's narrower ad-tech operations/capabilities as an independent player against the deep-pocketed ad-tech giants.

Worsening the ongoing execution/industry/demand headwinds, TTD has further fed the pessimism with an underwhelming FQ4'25 revenue guidance of $840M (+13.3% YoY) against a year ago at +22.2% YoY, with it underscoring its painfully decelerating growth profile.

Assuming a further erosion in its advertiser base moving forward, I am of the opinion that TTD may report an underwhelming FY2026 performance as more of its advertisers migrate to its competitors' walled garden platforms.

TTD's Reversal Still Possible

On the other hand, it is important to note that eMarketer already expects CTV ad sales to grow from $28.79B in 2024 to $46.89B in 2028, expanding at a CAGR of +12.9% - with it underscoring an increasingly larger TAM for an expanding group of ad-tech players.

Given that "98.4% of those dollars go to video ads, solidifying its place as one of the fastest-growing major ad channels in the US," I am of the opinion that TTD may remain somewhat relevant despite the challenging market environment.

This is especially true as the management has reported a robust customer retention rate at "over 95% during the third quarter, as it has for the past 11 consecutive years," with it implying minimal erosion risks to its ad-tech prospects as of FQ3'25.

While it remains to be seen how FQ4'25 may develop as AMZN's DSP partnerships ramp up, market reports are already highlighting TTD's increasingly leaning on pricing negotiations "with 1% to 2% reductions or post-auction discounts available if agencies hit quarterly spend thresholds."

This development implies the management's willingness to engage with its advertiser base despite the risks of a sequentially impacted top/bottom-line performance, as advertisers seemingly gain "more leverage at a time when DSP fees are under heightened scrutiny."

At the same time, TTD has highlighted how 85% of its client base has transitioned from Solimar to Kokai, as the new platform also drives improved acquisition trends with lower costs - with it directly contrasting bearish market reports:

One of the most influential data analytics companies in the world recently ran a competitive test between The Trade Desk and a major walled garden, which offered to run campaigns with 0 fees. But because we're objective, data-driven and focused on measurable outcomes, we won the opportunity, securing about $20 million more in incremental spend through the end of 2025. (Seeking Alpha)

TTD's Cheap Valuations Present Two Stories

The Consensus Forward Estimates

The consensus forward estimates appear rather promising for now, with TTD still expected to report a decent top/bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +16.6%/+14.1% through FY2027, despite the notable deceleration from the 5Y historical growth profile at +29.9%/+35.1%, respectively.

TTD Valuations

Most importantly, TTD's maturing growth cadence has already been priced into its consistently discounted FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 21.73x, compared to the last Buy article levels of 29.06x, the 1Y mean of 39.42x, and the 5Y mean of 71.51x.

A similar trend may be observed in the reasonably valued estimated 3Y PEG non-GAAP ratio of 1.54x, based on the FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 21.73x and the consensus 3Y adj EPS estimates of +14.1%, compared to the 5Y mean of 2.59x.

When comparing TTD's FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 21.73x and the estimated 3Y PEG non-GAAP ratio of 1.54x to its advertising/ad-tech peers, including:

  • DSP/SSP - Meta (META) at 26.34x/2.25x,
  • DSP/SSP - AMZN at 32.51x/1.62x,
  • DSP/SSP - GOOG at 30.25x/1.78x,
  • DSP/SSP - Unity (U) at 52.94x/9.98x,
  • DSP/SSP - AppLovin (APP) at 65.55x/1.93x, and
  • SSP - Magnite (MGNI) at 16.40x/1.18x, respectively,

it is apparent that TTD offers a compelling value Buy opportunity if its existing advertiser base stays on its ad-tech platform and eventually triggers its renewed profitable growth trends, as per consensus forward estimates.

Otherwise, it goes without saying that the stock's cheap valuations may present a potential value trap if the departure of its advertiser base proves permanent and their performance metrics are underwhelming compared to expectations/estimates.

Only time may tell.

TTD Downgraded To A Hold

TTD 5Y Stock Price

It goes without saying that TTD's 5Y price chart appears extremely brutal, with the stock losing all of its gains while notably breaching the established 5Y floor of $41s by the time of writing.

TTD Technical Indicators

It remains to be seen when a bounce may occur as well, with TTD's oversold RSIs and the moderating trading volumes implying a further deterioration in its stock price momentum pending any positive catalysts.

It goes without saying that the speculative stock price recovery directly hinges on Kokai's ability to retain its advertiser base without overly diluting the profit margins, barring which, the stock may potentially further retrace from current levels and retest the February 2020 peak of $30s in the near-term.

Pending further clarity on TTD's performance in the upcoming FQ4'25 earnings call sometime in February 2026, I am of the opinion that the stock may remain rather volatile in the near term, with the consistently moderating valuations and the loss of its 5Y stock price gains highlighting the inherent lack of bullish support worsened by the elevated short interest ratio of 11.05%.

Combined with the underwhelming outcome to my proven-to-be-wrong buy calls over the past three Buy articles, I am downgrading the stock to a Hold here.

It may be more prudent to observe from the sidelines for now.

data.title: What Moved Markets This Week
data.description:
Listen on the go! A daily podcast of Wall Street Breakfast will be available by 8:00 a.m. on Seeking Alpha, iTunes, Spotify.

Up for a challenge? Test your knowledge on the biggest events in the investing world over the past week. Take the latest Seeking Alpha News Quiz and see how you stack up against the competition.

Wall Street kicked off December with a volatile first week of trading. The three major averages saw range-bound moves in each session but eventually ended up positive for the week on Friday, in a pattern that matches historical trading trends during this holiday time of the year.

The biggest news of the week was undoubtedly Netflix's (NFLX) announcement to acquire Warner Bros. Discovery's (WBD) film and television studios and streaming assets in a deal with a total enterprise value of about $82.7B. The blockbuster acquisition was met with varying reactions, from analysts calling it a "very risky" move to Sen. Elizabeth Warren (D-MA) terming the deal an "anti-monopoly nightmare."

Investors also kept an eye on this week's economic calendar for labor market and inflation updates. Data showed U.S. private employment unexpectedly falling in November, but that was countered by better-than-expected initial jobless claims and job cut readings. Meanwhile, the Federal Reserve's preferred inflation gauge fell to its lowest Y/Y level since May. All in all, the data reinforced expectations for another interest rate cut by the central bank next week.

For the week, the S&P (SP500) added +0.3%, while the blue-chip Dow (DJI) gained +0.5%. The tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) advanced +0.9%. Read a preview of next week's major events in Seeking Alpha's Catalyst Watch.

data.title: QDF: Solid Factor Mix, Yet A Few Performance Disappointments, Unappealing Dividend Yield
data.description:
Today I would like to revisit the FlexShares Quality Dividend Index Fund ETF (QDF), a passively managed exchange-traded fund mixing quality and dividend factors.

It has been a while since I assessed this vehicle (the previous time was in May 2024, to be precise), and it, of course, deserves a fresh look, as the performance it has delivered since then is worthy of attention, plus its portfolio as of December 2025 is in numerous aspects different from that I previously dissected.

In the May 2024 article, I pointed out that QDF represents "...an underperforming strategy that delivered an unsatisfying downside capture ratio," and this concern was the primary reason why I remained neutral despite QDF having quite a healthy factor mix. Sadly, there has not been any notable improvement here, and QDF's performance is still not as robust as I would like it to be. And considering its quite high net expense ratio of 0.37% and a dividend yield of just 1.66%, I see no justification for a rating upgrade.

QDF Key Facts

  • Inception: December 14, 2012
  • AUM: $2.03 billion, as of December 5
  • Dividend Yield TTM: 1.66%, as of December 5
  • Expense Ratio: 0.37%
  • 5-Year Annualized Return (December 2020–November 2025): 13.50% vs. 14.37% of the iShares Russell 1000 ETF (IWB)

QDF Investment Strategy Recap

As we know from its website, QDF has been tracking the Northern Trust Quality Dividend Index since its inception in December 2012.

As described on page 2 of the summary prospectus, the index calibration process starts with the Northern Trust 1250 Index. In the first step, "...those securities that rank in the lowest quintile of quality based on a proprietary scoring model, as well as those which do not pay a dividend" are filtered out. The model that is at the heart of the process has three pillars, namely management efficiency, profitability, and cash flow, all of which I like and find relevant for a quality- and dividend-focused strategy.

In the next step, the optimization process is applied, with the purpose of delivering on three targets:

  • Boost the "overall quality score" compared to the Northern Trust 1250 Index,
  • reach an "aggregate dividend yield" higher than that of the parent index,
  • and "achieve the desired beta target."

Other parameters that are incorporated in the optimization process are

...sector, industry group, single-security weight, liquidity and turnover constraints to assist in reducing the Underlying Index’s overall active risk exposure to any one single factor.

Owing to the quarterly reconstitutions of the underlying index, QDF has a comparatively high turnover of 38%.

QDF Performance Analysis: Still Lagging IVV, IWB

In my view, the main issue with QDF is that while it does capture a sizable upside of the iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV) and the iShares Russell 1000 ETF, it still cannot outcompete these ETFs. For clarity, I added IWB since it tracks the fund's benchmark.

For instance, since my May 2024 article, QDF has delivered a rather competitive return, yet it has still trailed the S&P 500 index.

Looking at the January 2013–November 2025 period, we see quite a few issues.

  • QDF underperformed IWB and IVV by 2.15% and 2.47%, respectively.
  • To give a bit more color on that, my calculations show that QDF trailed IWB in 8 out of 12 calendar years it has in the books, with the median annual active return at -2.39%.
  • It had a slightly lower standard deviation than IWB but higher than IVV.
  • While focused on quality, which should have, at least in theory, improved risk metrics, QDF delivered the weakest maximum drawdown in the group (March 2020), as well as the softest risk-adjusted returns (measured using the Sharpe and Sortino ratios).
  • And ultimately, while it captured less than 93% of IVV's upside, its downside capture ratio was at 101.45%, a figure I consider unsatisfactory for a quality-oriented strategy.

QDF Portfolio Analysis

As of December 3, we see 120 equities in the QDF portfolio, with the key ten accounting for 38.9% of the net assets. Since May 2024, the portfolio has undergone rather deep adjustments, as only 67 equities (68.1% weight) have retained their place in it. Interestingly, Apple Inc. (AAPL) remains the key holding, with an 8.85% weight (8.2% as of May 9, 2024).

Key Ten Companies

QDF has a substantial overlap with the S&P 500 ETF (over 86%, as per my calculations), and all its key ten companies can be found in the IVV portfolio.

Stock Weight in QDF, as of December 3 Weight in IVV, as of December 3 Sector Market Cap, $ Billion
Apple Inc. 8.85% 7.20% Information Technology 4,198.7
NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) 6.18% 7.48% Information Technology 4,363.9
Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) 4.65% 6.06% Information Technology 3,550.7
Broadcom Inc. (AVGO) 4.35% 3.06% Information Technology 1,797.4
AbbVie Inc. (ABBV) 2.99% 0.69% Health Care 406.9
Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) 2.84% 0.84% Health Care 494.7
Cisco Systems, Inc. (CSCO) 2.34% 0.53% Information Technology 307.2
The Procter & Gamble Company (PG) 2.26% 0.59% Consumer Staples 342.8
Alphabet Inc. (GOOGL) 2.26% 3.17% Communication Services 3,862.3
General Electric Company (GE) 2.20% 0.52% Industrials 304.3
Alphabet Inc. (GOOG) 1.81% 2.56% Communication Services 3,862.3

Created by the author using data from the ETFs and Seeking Alpha. Market cap data as of December 4

QDF Sector Mix

Compared to the S&P 500 ETF, QDF is overweight in six out of eleven GICS sectors, key among them being real estate.

At the same time, it is notably underweight in communication and consumer discretionary, among others.

QDF Factor Mix

My calculations show that QDF is confidently ahead of IVV on only one front, and it is value. As to quality, the situation here is not completely certain, as a few metrics illustrate that IVV has an edge, but QDF is sporting a higher weighted average Return on Total Capital and adjusted WA Return on Equity. Unsurprisingly, considering its value tilt, growth characteristics are weaker than IVV's, yet they are, of course, not completely lackluster, as, for instance, a weighted average forward EPS growth rate of 16.3% looks quite healthy.

We also see the QDF portfolio having slightly lower weighted average beta coefficients, which I believe is consistent with it having weaker momentum characteristics than IVV (i.e., allocation to stocks with a B- Quant Momentum grade or higher).

QDF Portfolio Dividend Characteristics

When it comes to dividend characteristics, the QDF portfolio is ahead of the S&P 500 ETF in every aspect.

Metric QDF (Holdings as of December 3) IVV (Holdings as of December 3)
Dividend Yield TTM (weighted average) 1.98% 1.13%
Dividend Growth 3Y (weighted average) 12.32% 8.78%
Dividend Growth 5Y (weighted average) 9.94% 8.04%
Dividend Safety B- or higher 77.65% 70.49%
Dividend Growth B- or higher 77.73% 68.74%
Dividend Yield B- or higher 31.19% 12.26%
Dividend Consistency B- or higher 65.88% 55.07%

Calculated by the author using data from the ETFs and Seeking Alpha. Financial data as of December 4

However, the ETF itself has a TTM dividend yield of just 1.66% and an SEC-subsidized yield of 1.61%. This is, of course, far from impressive, considering IVV is yielding 1.13% (an SEC yield was at 1.04% as of October 31).

Final Thoughts

To conclude, QDF does not appeal to me either from the dividend yield (it is rather compressed) or performance perspectives. Its active returns (with IWB used as a benchmark) have been rather bleak, and risk metrics are not within the range of what I would call satisfactory. Sure, the factor mix is mostly solid, but this is not enough for a rating upgrade. In all, QDF is a vehicle that does deserve attention, but a Buy rating is excessive.

data.title: MSTY, BLOX, IWMI: Building A High-Income Portfolio
data.description:
I've been writing a lot about covered call ETFs lately, as they're quickly becoming a favorite for many investors seeking a boost in income from their ETF holdings.

Although it's important to understand how covered call ETFs work and include both those with a group of individual underlying assets and those with a basket of underlying assets, it's just as important to build a portfolio of ETFs using a strategy that has a safe foundation of ETFs, a medium-growth grouping of ETFs, and finally, those that provide ultra-growth potential that increase your weekly and monthly income to significant levels.

To successfully do that, it's necessary to first lay out the income goals we have, and from there, put together a portfolio built to reach those goals utilizing a strategy that fits your risk tolerance while taking advantage of the strength covered call ETFs have inherently in them.

In this article I'll go through several things that will help investors achieve their income goals while mitigating much of the risk.

One thing to keep in mind is this is the strategy I'm using to boost my ETF income; it's not necessarily meant to be a cookie-cutter strategy for everyone to use.

For example, there are many different goals investors have in relationship to generating income, different age brackets that impact how much risk to take on, and what the various weightings of the ETFs held in a portfolio will have.

I stand by the strategy I'm writing about and utilizing in my own investment journey, but it definitely is flexible and will be tweaked in various ways in alignment with the readers' personal goals.

Basis of the strategy

Every investor will need to first determine what their income goals are, not only for covered call ETFs but for their overall portfolio. Without that in place, it's close to impossible to develop a portfolio that will meet any goal, for the obvious reason that that there is no specific goal in place.

Once a very specific number is decided upon, along with the reasons for that income number, then we can go on to how to put the portfolio together to meet them.

I do want to mention here that not all portfolios as a whole are only going to have income-generating ETFs as the entirety of the portfolio. Consequently, we should separate those that are growth holdings from those that are invested in to meet our income goals.

Another element to take into consideration is what the floor of the distribution or dividend range you prefer, and from there add the medium- and ultra-yielding ETFs.

Last, as far as the basis of an income strategy, I'm aware that questions can arise in regard to if you have some extremely safe stocks or REITs that are already generating a consistent but low revenue stream.

In that case, I would obviously keep those as part of the weighting parameters of your ETF income portfolio, as long as it's understood that it'll lower the yield percentage of the overall income portfolio, as well as the weekly, monthly, or quarterly dividends or distributions the portfolio produces.

I know this is an article focusing on covered call ETFs, but if you're comfortable with a current holding that generates income, there should be no pressure to feel like you have to sell those off in order to have only covered call ETFs in your income portfolio.

3 levels of ETFs to consider

Here are the three buckets I consider when building my income portfolio: safe and foundational 10% to 25%; moderate returns of about 25% to 50%; and high-yield producers of 50% and above.

For those of you not too familiar with ultra-yield ETFs, those numbers may appear to be surreal, but there in fact are a growing number of these types of ETFs that do generate these types of returns.

Keep in mind that this is my portfolio strategy and not one I recommend you follow unless your goals are very similar to my goals. Build your own portfolio based upon your specific goals.

That said, I do think it's instructive to consider the three-bucket approach to building an income portfolio.

Specific weightings

The key to lowering risk with a high-yield income portfolio is to mitigate risk by keeping your weightings within each bucket at levels that don't overexpose the portfolio to any one ETF vastly underperforming.

With that in mind, my thought is the ETFs generating income from 10% to 25% should represent approximately 50% of a portfolio. That's a rough estimate because the ETFs can increase and decline in value to the point of moving above or below the 50% level by a few percentage points.

Concerning ETFs generating what I consider modest returns from 25% to 50%, I would have them represent about 30% of the portfolio, and the ultra-yielders above 50% would reflect about 20% of the overall portfolio.

Breaking it down to specific ETFs, those in the safe bucket could be in the 5% range each, the moderate ETFs each in the 3% range, and the high yielders would be around 2% over the overall weighting within the portfolio.

As mentioned above, the dynamic movement of the price range of the ETFs could move them in and out of their desired weightings, and at that time doing some rebalancing would be in order.

MSTY, BLOX, IWMI as proxies

To get an idea of what types of ETFs would represent the safe, moderate, and high-yield buckets, I'll use YieldMax MSTR Option Income Strategy ETF (MSTY), Nicholas Crypto Income ETF (BLOX), and NEOS Russell 2000 High Income ETF (IWMI) as proxies for each bucket. We'll start with IWMI.

The premise behind IMWI is to invest in ETFs that seek to track the Russell 2000 index, along with using call options to generate monthly income. At the time of my writing this article, the ETF had a yield of 13.80%. I would have IMWI or a similar ETF have near a 5% weighting in the portfolio.

For BLOX, it invests in crypto-related assets, companies, ETPs or ETFs. One different thing in regard to BLOX is it first seeks to build capital appreciation, with income generation being the secondary purpose of the fund. Its top three holdings include VanEck Bitcoin ETF, Fidelity Wise Origin Bitcoin Fund, and iShares Ethereum Trust ETF. Combined, they represent over 28% of its total holdings.

It pays out a distribution yield of about 35% as I write. For BLOX and similar ETFs, I would have a weighting of around 3%.

MSTY is among the top ETFs as far as distributions go. It, of course, has as its underlying Strategy Inc. (MSTR). That makes it more volatile than IMWI and BLOX because they include a basket of underlying holdings.

For ETFs like MSTY, I wouldn't have it be much over 2% at most in the portfolio in order to reduce risk. The TTM yield for MSTY at the time I'm writing is a hefty 237.79%. That percentage has been going down as MSTR has taken a hit because of the Bitcoin correction.

The point is these types of ETFs reflect what could be placed in each bucket of the income-generating portfolio built for the purpose of building a long-term income stream that performs, overall, at high levels.

Conclusion

A lot of things I see written or talked about with high-yield income ETFs tend to focus primarily on the individual ETF outside of an investing strategy. I've heard more than one investor say they're all in on one ETF or another. That, while having a lot of strong returns when the market is treating them well, over the long haul that will eventually disappoint those using that strategy.

Most of those implementing that strategy focus primarily on the ultra-high yielders, which, of course, cannot sustainably generate the type of income they think will last over a prolonged period of time.

That's why thinking in terms of a specific income ETF holding within the context of an overall portfolio is, I believe, the best way to take advantage of their strengths without exposing oneself to unnecessary risk.

While we need to research and understand these types of ETFs, it's just as important to consider each one as part of a larger whole. Those that do that should significantly increase their income while protecting the risk levels inherent in these types of income-generating ETFs.

data.title: Affirm: Strong Growth And Underwriting Discipline Signal A Buy (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Introduction:

Affirm (AFRM) has continued to solidify its positioning within the global lending ecosystem through its advanced models and expanded partnerships with various titans of the tech industry.

While the company does face a few potential headwinds related to its U.K. expansion ambitions and an evolving regulatory landscape, I also believe that the firm's ability to scale the top line while maintaining solid credit quality among its borrower base is highly impressive.

Despite the fact that shares have risen since my last coverage of Affirm back in July, they have underperformed the broader market, and I am now upgrading the stock from a HOLD to a BUY.

Business Description & Key Considerations:

Affirm is a company that helps its users pay for things through both 0% APR installment loans as well as interest-bearing loans. As of Q1 FY26, 72% of total gross merchandise value (GMV) sold on Affirm's platform was funded by interest-bearing loans, whereas the remaining 28% was from 0% APR installment loan options.

When a consumer applies for a loan, it is underwritten using Affirm's proprietary credit risk scoring model. This is a key competitive advantage for the business, as it not only prevents credit losses on retained loans but also increases trust among capital markets partners (who are crucial when it comes to helping fund the majority of loans the company makes).

Affirm's universal financing programs offer a variety of different payment plan options to consumers depending on how risky they are deemed to be based on these models. For instance, people who are clearly a low credit risk will have access to more generous financing options like non-interest bearing installment loans, whereas more risky borrowers may have to pay interest.

Some merchants also opt to subsidize consumer purchases in order to give them the option to take out a 0% APR installment loan (even if the borrower wouldn't have qualified for this option otherwise). This subsidy is paid by merchants to Affirm in the form of a higher transaction fee than typical exchanges.

The reason merchants are willing to do this is that it helps customers who may not have been willing to take out interest-bearing loans still buy goods. Therefore, businesses that sell higher-margin products may be willing to sacrifice some degree of profitability in order to boost the top and bottom lines in dollar terms.

Also, Affirm is highly adept at recognizing which shoppers should be subsidized and offered 0% loans to actually drive conversions. It is unwise for merchants to offer these deals universally, since there are likely many consumers who are willing to take out loans with interest in order to buy things.

For example, a user with a relatively solid credit profile may be willing to use other means to purchase something (such as a credit card or an interest-bearing Affirm loan), whereas others may instead forgo a purchase if they are not offered this cheap form of financing.

Affirm's edge here is that the company can utilize its adaptive checkout system to identify these specific users who are more likely to purchase things when given 0% loans based on both their shopping cart and credit profile statistics.

The company also offers many tools to these businesses that help to both stimulate demand and bring in new customers. Given all of these benefits of working with Affirm, active merchants on the platform grew nearly 30% YoY to 419K, which both boosts revenue and gives consumers more optionality.

Switching gears, it should be discussed how the company funds loans that it does not retain on its balance sheet. First, Affirm often creates securitization trusts which bundle many loans into a special purpose vehicle (SPV) that institutional investors can buy an ownership stake in.

Affirm has also been signing lots of forward flow agreements with investors, where these third-parties will outline the amount of risk they're willing to take on and Affirm will sell them loans that fit those criteria for a set period of time.

When doing these types of deals specifically, Affirm often enters into risk sharing agreements (RSAs) with investors, where Affirm is essentially offering to compensate the purchaser if actual losses on the loans exceed projected losses. On the flip side, if these loans perform better than expected, Affirm may be reimbursed by its investor partners.

As of the end of the most recent quarter, the total unpaid principal balance (UPB) of loans that are subject to RSAs was around $9.3 billion. These deals also all have a cap in place that limits Affirm's maximum downside in the event that lots of borrowers end up defaulting on these loans.

Also, between the end of FY23 and the most recent period, the total UPB of loans subject to RSAs has grown exponentially, but the implied cap percentage has actually declined by around 120 bps.

So, while this shows that Affirm does have more exposure to RSAs in dollar terms than it did in the past, it also implies that many investors are willing to purchase loans without strict deals in place to cap their own downside (which proves that many credit market participants trust Affirm's business model).

A key development over the past few years has been the growth in Affirm Card users, as those who activate cards tend to spend around 7x more than typical users. The company added an additional 500K Affirm cardholders in the most recent quarter, which helped bring the total to 2.8 million. Also, the percentage of total transaction volume on Affirm's platform from its card product has risen to around 11% (up 200 bps YoY).

I believe that continued growth in the credit card business is imperative for Affirm's future since consumers are most familiar with using this form of financing. Management appears to be aligned with my viewpoint, as the company has recently announced a new initiative to introduce cash flow underwriting to increase card penetration among consumers with limited borrowing history.

Now I'd like to discuss some of the key deals Affirm has struck with other large companies to enhance its competitive positioning. Back in September, it was announced that Affirm is expanding its integration with Apple Pay to allow consumers to take out loans using the iPhone interface.

I view this as important given that it should lead to sustained increases in transaction volume as users can now take out loans easily in stores that offer these tap-to-pay services (which have become ubiquitous across many developed markets).

Affirm has also expanded its partnerships with both Amazon and Shopify, which are critical since both firms operate massive consumer-facing platforms that get lots of traffic from users who are looking to purchase things. Both of these deals required Affirm to give its partners warrants; however, I believe this is a small price to pay for becoming embedded within their respective websites.

On the flip side, while these deals have paid dividends so far, they do also highlight the company's growing concentration risk. According to Affirm's most recent 10-K filing, its top five merchants and platform partners constituted 47% of total GMV, which is up substantially from just 32% in FY22. This should therefore be monitored closely, as failure to renew these partnerships or increases in GMV from these large businesses may spark a sell-off.

Finally, I think it's worth reviewing some of the risk factors that the business faces. On the most recent earnings call, CEO Max Levchin commented that "It is not [Affirm's] business to push or even entice consumers into more spending." I have two pieces of clear evidence that directly counter this claim from management.

First, on the same call, Levchin also stated that their 0% APR promotional programs are a more powerful way to "move the needle for the merchant ecosystem." To put this another way, he is essentially saying that this offer is designed to encourage people to make purchases that they may not otherwise have made.

Additionally, in this article published by Stripe, the company claimed that merchants who offered buy now, pay later (BNPL) options to consumers "saw up to a 14% increase in revenue, which resulted from increased conversion and higher average order values." Basically, consumers, when given this option, are both more likely to spend, and they also tend to buy more pricey goods too.

I think that if the public discourse surrounding BNPL loan providers continues to evolve and focus on the fact that these payment options are designed to increase discretionary spending among consumers who can't afford it, Affirm may face heightened regulatory scrutiny and perhaps lose members over time.

However, this risk will likely be greatly diminished over the next few years, given that the Trump administration has effectively barred the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB) from obtaining the funds it requires to run.

Another potential concern I have is related to Affirm's foray into the U.K. market. Management has claimed that consumers in this nation desire long term loans, given that many local banks aren't willing to approve these financing arrangements.

While I don't necessarily doubt this to be true, the company explicitly stated in its FY24 filing that it will have to adjust its risk algorithms because they're operating in a different regulatory environment and that Affirm will likely face competition from entrenched players who understand the market and its consumers better than they do.

Financials & Valuation:

Affirm reported very solid Q1 FY26 results, with total GMV growing 42% YoY to $10.8 billion and 0% APR monthly installment loan originations skyrocketing 74% to $1.4 billion.

However, the company's AOV declined by nearly 7% YoY to $260 in Q1, which contributed to revenue only growing by around 34% YoY to $933 million (a drop-off compared to the high-growth rate of GMV). It was noted in the recent 10-Q filing that a higher mix of low AOV purchases tends to decrease revenue as a percentage of GMV, which helps to explain the disparity.

Active consumers on the platform also grew 24% YoY and 5% sequentially to 24.1 million, with transactions per customer rising at a slightly slower pace (up around 20% YoY to 6.1). I view this as a natural development, given that a high-growth rate in new members should temporarily dilute the average transaction figure as consumers take some time to become power users.

Moving along, revenue less transaction costs as a percentage of total GMV for the quarter came out to 4.2%, which was up around 40 bps YoY. This metric is essentially the business's take rate, as it measures the income Affirm generates after accounting for the costs associated with helping to facilitate transactions. The company's goal is to have this metric come in at around 4% each quarter, since having a lower take rate should help to increase growth over the long run.

Turning to the balance sheet, of all of Affirm's retained loans (which amounted to nearly $7.2 billion), only around $408.1 million were past due (or around 5.7% of the entire portfolio). Out of these delinquent loans, nearly half are between 4 and 29 days past due, which is not incredibly alarming.

Additionally, only around $6.7 million of the portfolio is currently set to non-accrual status, meaning management does not view repayment as likely. These figures are very healthy overall and demonstrate to me that Affirm's credit models are working incredibly well in aggregate.

Furthermore, another major bright spot in Q1 was that the firm's provision for credit losses only grew 2% YoY despite a 19% increase in loans held for investment. This was largely due to the fact that the company managed to recover many loan balances during the quarter that had previously been charged off, which reduces the provisions expense.

Finally, loans modified due to consumers facing financial difficulties decreased to just 0.12% of total loan receivables outstanding, compared to 0.18% during the year-ago period. A similarly positive statistic was that the number of modified loans that were charged off over the TTM decreased by over 68% YoY to just $5.1 million, which again indicates that Affirm's credit-scoring systems are effectively filtering out high-risk borrowers.

Let's quickly discuss the valuation of Affirm to assess whether or not the stock presents a buying opportunity. Shares are currently trading at around 22.4x non-GAAP forward earnings, which is nearly double the sector median. However, I view this as justifiable given that the company is projected to grow its EPS by around 78% in FY26, which trumps the sector median growth rate of a little over 10%.

Additionally, Affirm is expected to see its top line expand by over 26% in FY26, which is again substantially higher than the sector average of just 7%. Premium growth expectations naturally translate to rich valuations, and Affirm is no exception to this rule. However, given the fact that Affirm trades below 14x FY28 earnings and the massive growth runway I view ahead, I believe shares remain undervalued.

Conclusion:

Affirm appears poised to continue growing at a rapid rate, as evidenced by recent financial results and expanded partnerships with many prominent consumer-facing platforms.

While the stock does trade at a premium valuation relative to the industry, the firm's projected future earnings growth along with strong underlying fundamentals suggests to me that further upside remains.

Given the stock's underperformance relative to the S&P 500 (SPY) since my last coverage, I am upgrading my rating on Affirm to a BUY.

data.title: WDI: Good For Income And Bear Markets
data.description:
Western Asset Diversified Income Fund (WDI) is a high-yielding, fixed-income fund that offers a nice 12% yield. I'm maintaining my Buy rating today, seeing it as a good example of the strengths of fixed income in more turbulent times.

Summary of Previous Thesis

I wrote about this fund the first time in April, when markets were scared about the impact of President Trump's Liberation Day tariffs. I wanted to spot fixed-income opportunities that might be better positioned for a bearish period. I made the following observation at the time:

The good news about such a portfolio is that most of the investments are secured by collateral, whether we're talking the CLOs, bank loans, MBS, or even many of the corporate notes. For WDI shareholders, this is a high-yield fixed-income fund that at least offers some safety of principal.

While many high-yield funds are in "junk" territory (and most of WDI's assets are below investment grade or not rated), these were more senior issues and therefore offered more safety.

With the significant diversification and a consistent history of paying monthly dividends, I thought WDI was not only good income at a 12% yield but a candidate for reinvestment to provide strong total returns in a bear market (specifically, a weaker macro environment due to tariffs).

While there was some risk from their own leverage, I felt the portfolio was good enough and the financing flexible enough (not marked to market) that

Recent Performance

The fund's portfolio has continued to pay, in spite of the earlier nervousness about the tariffs.

The monthly distribution of $0.1485 per share has continued, unabated, and the first distribution of 2026 has already been declared. In a review of their Q3 results, management felt there were "no notable detractors" in their holdings.

Many of the concerns about tariffs do not yet seem to have materialized into impacts on corporate financial results. This allowed for a recovery in equities, and so WDI has underperformed the broader market since then. Nevertheless, it's been almost 10% in less than a year, which isn't bad at all for fixed-income.

Current Portfolio

WDI's recent portfolio consists of $1.15B of assets, producing a 10.92% yield.

As in April, most of these are below investment grade or not rated, with a majority found in BB- and B-rated debt. The debt types are also quite varied, with high-yield credit (corporate debt securities) accounting for the largest portion at 44% of the portfolio (up from 40% when I wrote last time).

The recent holdings show the extent of diversification as well, with 420 holdings and nearly all of them accounting for less than a percent of the weighting.

The balance sheet continued to reflect only modest leverage, with $358M in debt through their credit agreement with Bank of New York Mellon (BK).

Outlook

I continue to think that WDI remains attractive, both for its income and relative safety.

During their Q3 outlook, management stated their view on interest rates going forward:

Fiscal vulnerabilities and persistent inflation may see the yield curve steepen further.

The Federal Reserve has made two 25-BPS cuts since my April article. As long-term rates remain flat, additional Fed cuts mean the curve is steeper. This can benefit WDI's net interest income, lowering the cost of capital from their credit agreement, while their portfolio continues to generate a similar yield. This will likely also benefit costs of capital for the issuers of their holdings, who likely pay higher costs on their bank debt as well.

There remain some risks from the ongoing effects of Trump's tariffs. Many companies continue to eat the costs that these tariffs pose, hesitant to pass them onto customers and risk losing business. While this is good for the consumer in most cases, corporate cash flow is tighter for it, which can impact a below-investment-grade portfolio like this one.

WDI trades at a slight NAV discount, about 3%, for net assets that are not excessively leveraged and for which the underlying principal is secured by the collateral of the companies. I think these help to mitigate the tariff risks, and that's why I maintain my Buy rating.

Conclusion

WDI is a slow-and-steady way to win at investing. It may not be as dramatic as equities on the upside, but the yield at 12% is good income. It also should compound like an equity index with reinvestment. That's why I think investors can find refuge in a fund like this, should weaker market periods persist.

data.title: 20 Ideal 'Safer' Monthly Paying Buyable December Dividend Equities And 80 Funds
data.description:
Foreword

All but five equities and all funds listed in this December monthly pay collection lived up to the ideal of paying annual dividends from a $1K investment exceeding their single share price. Here, in the MoPay batch, lie affordable (yet volatile and risky) bargains. The dogcatcher rule of thumb of one metric ($1,000.00 of invested annual dividend income > 1 share price) fits all!

Now, approaching six years beyond the 2020 Ides of March Covid market dip, and before the daddy of all other major pullbacks yet to come, the time to buy top-yield MoPay dogs may be now.

To learn the 20 Ideal MoPay equities paying "safer" dividends, see the conclusion of this article, in the Afterword. (You might even start at the afterword and work your way back to the front.)

Actionable Conclusions (1-10): Brokers Estimated Top Ten MoPay Equities Could Net 17.15% to 30.19% Gains By December 2026

Three of the ten top-yield MoPay stocks (shaded in the chart below) were verified as being among the top-ten gainers for the coming year based on analyst one-year target prices. Thus, the Dogcatcher yield-based strategy for this MoPay group, as graded by broker estimates this month, proved 30% accurate.

Estimated dividend payouts from $1000 invested in each of the ten highest-yielding stocks, plus analysts median 1-year target prices for those stocks, as reported by YCharts, produced the gain estimates below. (Note: Target prices from lone analysts were not counted.) Ten probable profit-generating trades to December 2026 were

PennantPark Floating Rate Capital (PFLT) was forecast to net $301.89 based on the median of target price estimates from 8 analysts, plus dividends, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 25% under the market as a whole.

Orchid Island Capital Inc. (ORC) netted $299.29 based on the median of target price estimates from 2 analysts, plus dividends, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 66% over the market as a whole.

Modiv Industrial (MDV) netted $283.59, based on price estimates from 6 analysts, plus dividends, less broker fees. A Beta number was not available for MDV.

Slate Grocery REIT (OTC:SRRTF) netted $283.28 based on the median of target price estimates from 2 analysts, plus dividends, The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 1% less than the market as a whole.

Gladstone Commercial (GOOD) netted $264.42 based on the median of target price estimates from 5 analysts, plus dividends, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 10% more than the market as a whole.

PennantPark Investment (PNNT) netted $213.67 based on the median of target price estimates from 8 analysts, plus dividends, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 38% under the market as a whole.

Stellus Capital Investment (SCM) netted $203.84 based on the median of target price estimates from 6 analysts, plus dividends less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 34% under the market as a whole.

Ellington Financial (EFC) netted $177.36 based on the median of target price estimates from 8 analysts, plus dividends, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 6% less than the market as a whole.

Apple Hospitality REIT (APLE) netted $175.06 based on the median of target price estimates from 8 analysts, plus dividends, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 16% under the market as a whole.

Dynex Capital Inc. (DX) netted $171.49 based on the median of target price estimates from 6 analysts, plus dividends, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 2% less than the market as a whole.

Average net gain in dividend and price was 23.74% on $1k invested in each of these ten MoPay stocks. This gain estimate was subject to average risk/volatility 2% less than the market as a whole.

The Dividend Dogs Rule

Stocks earned the "dog" moniker by exhibiting three traits: (1) paying reliable, repeating dividends, (2) their prices fell to where (3) yield (dividend/price) grew higher than their peers. Thus, the highest yielding stocks in any collection became known as "dogs." More specifically, these are, in fact, best called "underdogs".

December Monthly Pay Dividend Data

Three lists produce numerous actionable conclusions and several more unnumbered results. To draw these conclusions and results, September 29, 2025 closing prices and estimated annual dividends were referenced from YCharts. In the process, monthly pay equity (1) yield and (2) upside potential lists were compared and contrasted against (3) the high-yield (and higher-risk) MoPay CEICs/ETFs/ETNs list.

Monthly Pay Dividend Qualities

Quarterly, Semi-Annual and Annual dividend investors anxiously await announcements from a firm, fund, or brokerage to learn if their next dividend will be higher, lower, or paid at all.

Monthly pay stocks, funds, trusts, and partnerships inform the holder every four and one third weeks by check and/or statement. If the entity reduces or suspends a payment, the holder can sell out of the investment immediately to avoid future loss of income.

This advantage has been curtailed when companies suddenly cut monthly dividends to save cash. Numerous prominent MoPay firms declared dividend cuts between May and June, 2020, including: Oxford Square Capital Corp; Partners Real Estate Investment Trust; Orchid Island Capital Inc; Cross Timbers Royalty Trust; H&R Real Estate Investment Trust; BTB Real Estate Investment Trust; American Finance Trust Inc; Mesa Royalty Trust; Solar Senior Capital Ltd; Ellington Financial Inc; Dividend Select 15 Corp; Chesswood Group Ltd; Sabine Royalty Trust; TORC Oil & Gas Ltd; Freehold Royalties Ltd; ARC Resources Ltd; Inter Pipeline Ltd; San Juan Basin Royalty Trust; Ag Growth International.

Former MoPay top ten regular by yield. Bluerock Residential announced December 2019 it was retreating to quarterly dividend payments "in keeping with industry tradition." Bluerock Residential company has since gone private and is no longer listed publicly.

That “industry tradition” continued with Armour Residential REIT and Stellus Capital Investment Corp both transitioned to Q Pay in June, 2020. Within three months, however, both SCM and ARR returned to MoPay mode.

Top yield stock for July, 2018, June 2021, and December 2023, Orchid Island Capital, released this cautionary note with its monthly dividend announcements back in 2018: "The Company has not established a minimum distribution payment level and is not assured of its ability to make distributions to stockholders in the future."

ORC directors proceeded to back-up their words with actions, cutting the dividend from $0.14 to $0.11 in February, to $0.09 in March, to $0.08 in September, 2018, and to $0.055 for May, 2020. However, in August. 2020 ORC monthly dividend increased from $0.06 to $0.065 for September, October, and into 2021.

ORC, however, currently pays a $0.12 monthly dividend (falling from $0.16 in October 2023) and is again listed here.

The U.S. stock market MoPay segment is volatile, transitive, and recovering, and since 2024 has achieved recovery to near full strength. More trades in monthly pay equities are available from Canadian firms, many of which are also listed on U.S. OTC (pink sheet) exchanges. Active listed MoPays priced over $5 number 65 for December 2025.

List One:

US Exchange Traded MoPay Dividend Equities by Yield, Analyst Targets, & Annual Returns

Annual Yield

Top ten of these US exchange listed monthly pay dividend equities showing the best yields for December, represented three of the eleven Morningstar market sectors and included three Canadian Closed-end Investment ETFs:

One Real Estate representative placed first, Smart Stop Self Storage REIT (SSST) [1].

Thereafter five of the ten firms were from the Financial Services sector: Orchid Island Capital [2]; Armour Residential (ARR) [5]; PennantPark Investment Corp [6], Dynex Capital [9], and AGNC Investment (AGNC) [10]. Then one Industrial placed third, 2020 Bulkers (OTCPK:TTBKF) [3].

Finally, the three Canadian CEIC Exchange Traded Funds placed fourth, seventh and eighth, Dividend 15 Split Corp (OTCPK:DVSPF) [4]; Dividend Growth Split Corp (OTCPK:DDWWF)[7], and Canadian Banc Corp (OTCPK:CNDCF) [8], to complete the December MoPay top-ten equities list by yield.

Analyst Targets

Only twenty-three of these US exchange listed monthly pay dividend equities earned positive grades from analysts in late November. Those twenty-three occupied just two of the eleven Morningstar market sectors. The firms split 13, and 10 between the real estate and financials sectors, respectively. Two more in the financial and one in the energy sectors showed negative analyst target grades. Most of the top 65 were unrated by analysts

Annual Returns

Top ten of these US exchange listed monthly pay dividend equities showing the best returns into December, represented four of the eleven Morningstar market sectors, plus one ETF from Canada. Four of the ten firms were from the financial services sector; two were from real estate; one represented energy and one more in health care and the CEIC completed the December MoPay top-ten equities list based on one-year returns.

Yields, Targets, & Returns Summarized

One on the sixty-five December MoPay list finished in the top twenty by yields, targets, and returns, and all were rated IDEAL (with free cash flow yield exceeding dividend yield). The one was: Stellus Capital Investment.

List Two (A):

Monthly Pay Dividend Equities by Price Upsides

Results from YCharts, shown below, show 30 MoPay dividend stocks (as of the market closing price on December 2) compared with the median of analyst target prices one year out. The ten top stocks displayed 5.72% to 21.30% price upsides for the coming year.

Two (tinted) of ten on this top upside list were also members of the top ten list by yield.

Furthermore, the top five places on this upside list went to: Modiv Industrial [1], Slate Grocery REIT [2], PennantPark Floating Rate Capital [3], Gladstone Commercial [4], and Orchid Island Capital [5].

The lower level (by upside %) five were, Apple Hospitality [6], Stellus Capital Investment [7], Ellington Financial [8], Gladstone Capital Corp (GLAD) [9], and PennantPark Investment [10]

Price upside, of course, was defined as the difference between the current price and analyst target one-year median price targets for each stock.

Ten MoPay stocks showing the top-estimated price-gains into December, 2026 were gleaned from 30 selected by yield. Three to nine analysts have historically provided the most accurate median target price estimates.

List Two (B):

29 of 65 Monthly Pay Dividend Equities Show Negative Free Cash Flow Margins

A negative gap between dividend yield and free cash flow yield defines a cash-poor stock. That is, their dividend payout exceeds their cash on hand to pay dividends. Twenty-nine of sixty-five December MoPay so infected are considered unsafe to buy.

Remember this dogcatcher yield-based stock-picking strategy is contrarian. That means rooting for (buying) the underdog is productive when you don't already own those stocks. If you do hold those stocks, then you must look for opportune pull-backs in price to add to your position to best improve your dividend yield. Plenty of pull-back opportunities appear to be ahead. Dread the Fed, uncertainty, the toll of the trade tariff, Troll, and his Taco dealings. Wars and murder are also dreadful

List Three:

MoPay Dividend Closed End Investment Companies, Exchange Traded Funds, and Notes, by Yield

Eighty top monthly dividend paying Closed End Funds, Exchange Traded Funds and Notes listed below were culled from nearly 800 candidates. The top ten were determined by yields of 24.33% or greater, calculated as of October 29, All 80 show assets under management [AUM] greater than $100M and are priced above $10.00 per share.

The top-ten monthly-paying dividend investment companies, funds, & notes showing the biggest yields per YCharts data, featured TWO uncollateralized debt instruments [ETN],

EIGHT open-ended investment companies [ETFs], and NO closed-ended investment companies [CEICs].

The two top ten uncollateralized debt instruments (ETNs) placed third and seventh: UBS ETRACS Crude Oil Shares Covered Call ETN (USOI) [3], and UBS ETRACS Silver Shares Covered Call ETN (SLVO) [7]. The top eight open ended investment companies (ETFs) placed first, second , fourth to sixth, and eighth to tenth: REX AI Equity Premium Income ETF (AIPI) [1]; Roundhill Innov-100 0DTE Covered Call Strategy ETF (QDTE) [2]; Rex Fang & Innovation Equity Premium Income ETF (FEPI) [4]; Simplify Volatility Premium ETF (SVOL) [5]; YieldMax AAPL Option Income Strategy ETF (APLY) [5]; 2x Bitcoin Strategy ETF (BITX) [6]; ProShares Ultra Bitcoin ETF (BITU) [8]; iShares S&P 500 BuyWrite ETF (IVVW) [9]; iShares Investment Grade Corporate Bd BW Strat ETF (LQDW) [10], to complete the top-ten Exchange Traded Notes, Exchange Traded Funds, and Closed End Funds, into December 25-26.

Compare Equity To Fund Performance

Note that December 2025-26 top-ten equity dividends were now priced 11% below those of the ten funds. Last month they were 4% lower. Last June they were 1% higher. The price differences are caused by the outlandishly high dividends offered by a few of the top equities or funds.

This month the top fund dividend is at 33.65%, whereas the top equity pays just 21.95%. Nevertheless, equities are usually cheaper than funds because equities are not loaded with the high overhead costs of fund management. More middlemen muddle and mean more money.

Background and Actionable Conclusions

In June 2012 readers suggested the author include these funds, trusts, and partnerships in the MoPay article. A list of MoPay equities to buy and hold in September 2012 resulted from those reader suggestions supplemented with a high yield collection from here. That list was supplemented by an upside potential article in October and a upside vs. buy & hold in November. Another list factored in December 2012 reader comments.

A new decade of 2020 that began in January, and continued to February, March, April, early May, and mid-May, June, July, August, September, October, November, December. The progression continued in 2021 in January, February, March, April, May, June, July, August, September, October, November, and December. And carried-on in 2022, January, February, March, April, May, June/July, August, September, October, November, December, and again, in 2023, January, February, March, April. May, June, July, August, September, October, and December.

Then, in 2024, we continued in January, February, March, April, May, June, July, August, September, October, November and December we continued.

This year, in 2025 January, February, March, April, May, June, August, September, October, November, and now December, we still compare and contrast MoPay equity upside potential to the yield (and higher risk/volatility) should any investor be tempted to buy and hold Closed End Investments, Exchange Traded Funds, or Notes.

Yield Metrics Showed A 17.79% Advantage For The Five Lowest-Priced Of Ten High-Yield MoPay Equities For December

Ten monthly pay stocks were ranked by yield. Those results, verified by YCharts, Seeking Alpha, and YahooFinance, produced the following charts.

Actionable Conclusions: Analysts Estimated 5 Lowest Priced of Top Ten High Yield MoPay Dividend Stocks (11) Would Produce 20.61% VS. (12) 17.5% Net Gains from All Ten by December, 2026

$5000 invested as $1k in each of the five Lowest priced stocks in the top ten MoPay dividend dog kennel by yield were predicted by analyst 1 year targets to deliver 17.79% more net gain than $5,000 invested as $500 each in all ten. The fourth lowest-priced MoPay dividend dog, Orchid Island Capital, was predicted to deliver the best net gain of 29.93%.

Lowest priced five MoPay dividend stocks estimated for December were: Dividend 15 Split; Dividend Growth Split; PennantPark Investment; Orchid Island Capital; Smart Self Storage, with prices ranging from $5.10 to $7.43.

Higher priced five MoPay dividend equities, estimated as of October 29, were: Canadian Banc; AGNC Investment; 2020 Bulkers; Dynex Capital; ARMOUR Residential , whose prices ranged from $9.86 to $17.30.

This distinction between five low-priced dividend dogs and the general field of ten reflects the "basic method" Michael B. O'Higgins employed for beating the Dow. The same technique, you now see, is today useful to find long-term rewarding dogs in the MoPay kennel.

The added scale of projected gains, based on analyst targets, contributed a unique element of "market sentiment" gauging upside potential. It provided a here and now equivalent of waiting a year to find out what might happen in the market. It is also the work analysts got paid big bucks to do.

Caution is advised, however, as analysts are historically 15% to 85% accurate on the direction of change and about 0% to 15% accurate on the degree of the change.

Gains/declines as reported do not factor-in any tax problems resulting from dividend, profit, or return of capital distributions. Consult your tax advisor regarding the source and consequences of "dividends" from any investment.

Afterword

If somehow you missed the suggestion of the stocks ripe for picking at the start of this article, here is a reprise of the list at the end:

November’s 65 MoPay Equities List

(Alphabetical by Ticker Symbol)

All but five equities and all but three funds listed in this September collection of monthly-paying dividend dogs live up to the ideal of annual dividends from $1K invested exceeding their single share price. Here in the MoPay collection lie affordable yet volatile and risky bargains.

December 24 “Safer” MoPays of 65 Equities Listed

(Dividend from $1K Invested > Share Price)

Now, nearly six years beyond the 2020 Ides of March dip, and before more pull-backs yet to come, the time to buy top yield MoPay dogs may now be at hand.

There are more to choose from. This month’s list of 65 equities showed a maximum yield of 21.96%, resulting from setting the minimum price per share for selection at $5, and holding the minimum yields to a range of 5% to 25%. When bears are back, bargains reappear.

Buy Any of 20 Ideal ‘Safer’ December US MoPay Equities

Early-December 2025, 20 of the 24 “Safer” MoPay Equities were tagged “IDEAL” for dividends as they showed positive one-year returns, free-cash-flow-yields greater-than dividend-yields, and dividends from $1,000 invested exceeding single share prices.

Stocks listed above were suggested only as decent starting points for your MoPay dividend stock purchase or sale research process. These were not recommendations.

Disclaimer: This article is for informational and educational purposes only and should not be construed to constitute investment advice.
